title: September 11 attacks
summary: On the morning of 11 September 2001, nineteen operatives of the militant Islamist network al-Qaeda hijacked four transcontinental airliners on the east coast of the United States. Two were flown into the twin towers of the World Trade Center in New York; a third into the west facade of the Pentagon outside Washington; a fourth, its passengers in revolt, crashed into a field near Shanksville, Pennsylvania. In 102 minutes 2,977 people were killed. The attacks were the deadliest ever carried out on American soil, triggered a global 'war on terror' and two US-led invasions, and reshaped politics, security, and law in most of the world.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The plot's architect, Khalid Sheikh Mohammed, first pitched a scheme to fly hijacked aircraft into American buildings to Osama bin Laden, the Saudi-born leader of al-Qaeda, in 1996. Bin Laden had spent the late 1980s helping to fund the Afghan mujahideen against the Soviet occupation; after 1988 he built al-Qaeda ('the base') as a global jihadist network. In February 1998 he issued a fatwa calling on Muslims 'to kill the Americans and their allies, civilian and military, ... in any country'. Six months later, on 7 August 1998, al-Qaeda operatives bombed the US embassies in Nairobi and Dar es Salaam, killing more than 200. The Clinton administration retaliated with cruise-missile strikes on Sudan and Afghanistan and indicted bin Laden, but he remained under the protection of the Taliban regime in Kandahar.

By late 1999 the September 11 plot, referred to inside al-Qaeda as the 'planes operation', had been approved. Its selection of targets, the World Trade Center as a symbol of American economic power, the Pentagon of military power, and, according to the 9/11 Commission, the US Capitol of political power, reflected a deliberately theatrical strategy. Fifteen of the nineteen chosen operatives were Saudi citizens; the others were from Egypt, the United Arab Emirates, and Lebanon. Four of them, the designated pilots, including the Egyptian Mohamed Atta, had spent 1999 and 2000 in Hamburg. They travelled to the United States in 2000 and enrolled in flight training in Florida and Arizona, learning to steer but not to take off or land large commercial jets. The remaining fifteen 'muscle' hijackers arrived in 2001.

Al-Qaeda's plans did not go entirely unnoticed. In the summer of 2001 a stream of intelligence 'chatter' warned of an imminent, spectacular attack; a 6 August Presidential Daily Brief to George W. Bush was titled 'Bin Laden Determined to Strike in US'. But warnings were fragmentary, the intelligence agencies did not share them well, and no specific date or target was known. Two of the future hijackers had been identified as al-Qaeda associates by the CIA but had not been placed on airline watch lists. On the morning of 11 September 2001 the four hijack teams passed through security at Boston Logan, Newark, and Washington Dulles airports without difficulty.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
American Airlines Flight 11, a Boeing 767 bound from Boston to Los Angeles, departed Logan International Airport at 07:59 Eastern Daylight Time with 92 people aboard. Around fifteen minutes into the flight the five hijackers, led by Mohamed Atta, killed at least one flight attendant, forced their way into the cockpit, and turned the aircraft south toward New York. At 08:46:40 Flight 11 struck the north face of the North Tower of the World Trade Center between the 93rd and 99th floors at some 750 kilometres per hour. All aboard, and an unknown number of workers in the impact zone, were killed instantly.

United Airlines Flight 175, another 767, departed Logan for Los Angeles at 08:14. It was hijacked in similar fashion by a team led by Marwan al-Shehhi. At 09:03:02, live on the television cameras that had converged on the burning North Tower, Flight 175 banked steeply into the South Tower, striking floors 77 to 85 and killing all 65 aboard. Within minutes it was clear the United States was under coordinated attack.

American Airlines Flight 77, a Boeing 757 bound from Washington Dulles to Los Angeles, was hijacked between 08:51 and 08:54; at 09:37:46 it flew into the west face of the Pentagon at ground level, killing all 64 aboard and 125 people inside the building. The Federal Aviation Administration ordered a nationwide ground stop of all civil aviation at 09:42, the first in American history.

United Airlines Flight 93, a 757 bound from Newark to San Francisco, took off 41 minutes late at 08:42 and was hijacked around 09:28. Its passengers, learning by air-phone of the earlier crashes, stormed the cockpit. At 10:03:11 Flight 93 dived into a reclaimed strip mine near Shanksville, in western Pennsylvania, killing all 44 aboard. The 9/11 Commission concluded that the intended target had been the US Capitol or the White House.

In New York the fires ignited by the impacts weakened the steel frames of the twin towers. The South Tower collapsed at 09:59, fifty-six minutes after being hit; the North Tower followed at 10:28. Vast clouds of pulverised concrete swept through lower Manhattan; the streets were coated in white ash. At 17:20 the 47-storey 7 World Trade Center, damaged by falling debris and fires no longer fought by an overwhelmed fire department, also collapsed. In total 2,977 people from more than ninety countries were killed, along with the 19 hijackers. Among the dead were 343 members of the New York Fire Department and 71 law-enforcement officers. The material damage in New York alone was later estimated at over 10 billion dollars.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
President George W. Bush, in Sarasota, Florida, that morning for a school visit, was taken aboard Air Force One and flown, via bases in Louisiana and Nebraska, back to Washington. Addressing the nation from the Oval Office that evening, he declared, 'We will make no distinction between the terrorists who committed these acts and those who harbor them.' The following days brought a global outpouring of sympathy: 'Nous sommes tous americains' ran the front page of Le Monde; NATO invoked its Article 5 collective-defence clause for the first time on 12 September; the UN Security Council passed Resolution 1368.

Within weeks the Bush administration had presented an ultimatum to the Taliban regime in Kabul demanding that it hand over bin Laden. When it refused, the United States, joined by Britain and the Afghan Northern Alliance, began the war in Afghanistan on 7 October 2001. Kabul fell in November; the Taliban were driven from power by December; bin Laden escaped into Pakistan. The US Congress passed the Authorization for Use of Military Force on 14 September and, on 26 October, the USA PATRIOT Act, dramatically expanding surveillance powers. The Department of Homeland Security was created in 2002. A prison for suspected terrorists was opened at the US naval base at Guantanamo Bay in Cuba on 11 January 2002, holding hundreds without trial.

In March 2003 the Bush administration, with British support, invaded Iraq on the grounds that Saddam Hussein was developing weapons of mass destruction and had links to al-Qaeda. Neither claim proved substantiated. The war overthrew Saddam within weeks but triggered a long insurgency, sectarian civil war, and ultimately the emergence of the Islamic State group. The 'war on terror' also involved extraordinary rendition, secret CIA 'black sites' where prisoners were tortured, drone strikes across Pakistan, Yemen, and Somalia, and mass electronic surveillance of American and foreign communications, revealed by Edward Snowden in 2013.

Bin Laden himself was killed by US Navy SEALs in a raid on a compound in Abbottabad, Pakistan, on 2 May 2011, almost a decade after the attacks. The war in Afghanistan continued, however, until the Taliban's return to Kabul on 15 August 2021 amid the chaotic American withdrawal ordered by President Joe Biden.

At Ground Zero in New York, cleanup took eight months and removed 1.8 million tons of debris. The National September 11 Memorial, twin reflecting pools set in the footprints of the fallen towers with the names of all victims inscribed around their rims, opened on the tenth anniversary in 2011; the accompanying museum opened in 2014. The 541-metre One World Trade Center, deliberately built to a symbolic height of 1,776 feet, opened for occupancy in 2014.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The September 11 attacks are widely regarded as the pivotal event that ended the post-Cold War 'holiday from history' and defined a new era of transnational conflict. For a generation of Americans they became the equivalent of Pearl Harbor: a moment before which and after which the country was, in various ways, not the same. Airport security, immigration policy, the architecture of federal government, and constitutional debates over surveillance and executive war powers were all transformed.

Globally, the response reshaped much of the Islamic world. The Iraq war destabilised the Middle East, contributed to the sectarian violence that spilled into Syria after 2011, and enabled the temporary rise of the Islamic State's territorial 'caliphate' in 2014-2019. In Afghanistan two decades of Western intervention ended with the Taliban's return to power. Muslim communities in Western countries faced a lasting climate of suspicion; hate crimes rose sharply after 2001 and again after subsequent attacks in Madrid (2004), London (2005), and Paris (2015).

The attacks changed civil-military relations. Special operations forces and armed drones, marginal before 2001, became central instruments of US foreign policy under presidents of both parties. Legal categories such as 'enemy combatant' and detention without trial at Guantanamo tested the boundaries of the laws of war; some detainees remained there more than two decades later.

Health costs continued to accumulate. More than 4,000 first responders and lower-Manhattan residents have died since 2001 of illnesses linked to exposure to the toxic dust at Ground Zero; more than 100,000 are enrolled in the World Trade Center Health Program. The financial cost of the 'war on terror', by an estimate of the Costs of War project at Brown University, has exceeded eight trillion dollars, with over 900,000 direct war deaths across the several theatres it opened.

For historians, September 11 marks less a beginning than a rupture: a moment at which a small, highly organised non-state group inflicted damage that reshaped a superpower's foreign policy and, through that, much of the world. That asymmetry, the possibility of small actors leveraging global systems of aviation, media, and networked ideology, is now a permanent condition of international security.

worldContext.text: In September 2001 the euro was three months from entering circulation as a physical currency, replacing twelve national currencies on 1 January 2002. The German government of Gerhard Schroder faced a general election the following year; Tony Blair was midway through his second term as British prime minister and would join the US-led wars in Afghanistan and Iraq. In the Balkans, NATO peacekeepers were still deployed in Bosnia and Kosovo. Russia under Vladimir Putin, in office since December 1999, offered rapid support to the US after 9/11, opening a brief window of cooperation.

China had been admitted to the World Trade Organization in November 2001, sealing its integration into the global economy; Jiang Zemin remained president. Japan, under Prime Minister Junichiro Koizumi, was still mired in its 'lost decade' of low growth. North Korea under Kim Jong-il was rebuilding its nuclear programme; it would withdraw from the Non-Proliferation Treaty in 2003 and test a device in 2006. South Korea's engagement with the North under Kim Dae-jung's 'Sunshine Policy' was in its later stages.

The African Union was being founded that year, formally launched at Durban in July 2002 to replace the Organisation of African Unity. Zimbabwe's controversial land-reform seizures under Robert Mugabe were accelerating. In Sierra Leone the civil war ended in January 2002 with a British-led intervention. Sub-Saharan Africa was in the depths of the HIV/AIDS crisis: on 5 September 2001 the World Health Organization estimated that 25 million people on the continent were HIV-positive.

Two days before the September 11 attacks, on 9 September 2001, al-Qaeda suicide bombers assassinated Ahmad Shah Massoud, the Afghan Northern Alliance commander and Taliban's principal opponent, evidently to weaken the resistance in advance of the American retaliation they knew was coming. India and Pakistan, both newly declared nuclear powers since 1998, were in a period of high tension after the Kargil conflict of 1999; a December 2001 attack on the Indian parliament would bring them close to war.

Australia under Prime Minister John Howard rapidly invoked the 1951 ANZUS Treaty and committed troops to Afghanistan and, in 2003, Iraq. The 'Tampa affair' of late August 2001, in which Howard's government refused entry to a Norwegian freighter carrying rescued asylum seekers, had reshaped Australian border policy in the weeks before 9/11 and helped Howard win re-election in November. New Zealand under Helen Clark supported the Afghanistan mission but declined to join the Iraq war.

figures.name: Osama bin Laden
figures.role: Founder and leader of al-Qaeda; approved and financed the September 11 plot; killed by US forces in Pakistan on 2 May 2011

figures.name: Khalid Sheikh Mohammed
figures.role: Chief operational architect of the September 11 plot; captured in Pakistan in 2003, held at Guantanamo Bay

figures.name: Mohamed Atta
figures.role: Lead hijacker; piloted American Airlines Flight 11 into the North Tower of the World Trade Center

figures.name: George W. Bush
figures.role: 43rd President of the United States; declared the 'war on terror' in response to the attacks

figures.name: Rudy Giuliani
figures.role: Mayor of New York City on 11 September 2001; led the immediate municipal response

images.caption: The burning twin towers of the World Trade Center seen across New York harbour from the Statue of Liberty, 11 September 2001.
images.credit: US National Park Service, via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: Aerial view of the Pentagon in Arlington, Virginia, showing the collapsed section of the west facade struck by American Airlines Flight 77 on 11 September 2001.
images.credit: TSGT Cedric H. Rudisill / US Air Force, via Wikimedia Commons
